turns.content: I'm new to this codebase and I thought traits just defined the interface without any implementation, so I was confused why there are methods with body text inside them.

That's a common misunderstanding. In Rust traits can include default implementations for methods you don't want to override immediately. When you define a trait method like fn process_data() and provide body text, it acts as the fallback logic.
So if I have a struct that implements this trait, does it always have to use the default implementation unless I explicitly override with my own code?
Exactly. Your struct will inherit the default behavior automatically unless you provide an explicit override. You can choose which implementations to keep and which to replace selectively.

Why does this even work this way? I just wrote a trait with some default methods, then in my struct I tried to override one of them by simply redeclaring the method body. My compiler immediately complained that these are duplicate definitions and I have to manually call super or something weird is happening.

That frustration is common when moving from C++ to Rust because Rust's trait system doesn't allow you to shadow a method definition just by writing it again. When your struct redeclares a method that already has an implementation in the trait, the compiler sees them as conflicting definitions unless you explicitly tell the derived impl which part of the trait body you are overriding and which parts you are inheriting.
So I can't just copy paste the method? What's the correct way to handle this situation where a trait has default logic but my struct needs to tweak it slightly?

You need to use the Super::method call pattern. When you implement your trait for your specific type in your impl block, you declare the method without providing a body and then inside that method's body you invoke Super::method. This tells the compiler exactly which default implementation you are keeping and allows you to add custom logic around it without creating duplicate definitions.
Wait, so I'm not allowed to provide a body at all? Does that mean every single method in my impl block has to be empty until I call Super?

Not necessarily, but for methods you are overriding from the trait you must follow the Super pattern. However, if your struct does not override a specific method and it already has a default implementation in the trait, you don't need to declare anything at all in your impl block for that specific method; Rust will automatically use the trait's default body.
